description: Armed conflict events, fatalities, and kidnapping incidents in Nigeria

sources.name: ACLED
sources.note: Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project

sources.name: TheCable / National Security Tracker
sources.note: Media analysis of CFR/ACLED data, 2023 annual figures

sources.name: National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)
sources.note: Official NHRC monthly and annual figures

sources.name: NBS Crime Experience Survey (2024)
sources.note: National household survey — broader definition than media-tracked data

data.note: Pre-Boko Haram peak. Jos crisis and sectarian violence.
Post-election violence ~800 killed. Boko Haram bombed UN HQ Abuja (Aug).
keyEvents: Boko Haram UN HQ bombing, Abuja

data.note: Boko Haram intensified attacks in northeast. ~1,200+ killed (HRW estimate).
State of emergency declared in Borno, Yobe, Adamawa.
keyEvents: State of emergency in 3 NE states

data.note: Deadliest year — Boko Haram killed ~6,000+. ACLED estimated ~10,800 total conflict fatalities.
Baga massacre (~2,000 killed, Jan). Military recaptured territory.
Boko Haram weakened but active. Niger Delta Avengers pipeline attacks.
Farmer-herder violence intensified in Middle Belt. ~2,000+ killed.
Farmer-herder violence surpassed Boko Haram fatalities. ~2,000+ killed in Middle Belt.
Banditry surged in northwest (Zamfara, Katsina, Kaduna). ISWAP gained strength.

keyEvents: #EndSARS protests and Lekki toll gate shooting (Oct 20)

data.note: Mass school kidnappings escalated. Boko Haram leader Shekau killed.
keyEvents: School kidnappings in Kaduna, Niger, Zamfara

data.note: Owo church attack. Kuje prison break. Kaduna-Abuja train kidnapping.
TheCable/CFR analysis: 3,841 killed by non-state actors, 4,243 kidnapped. Average 11 killed and 12 kidnapped daily. Northern region: 83.8% of killings.
keyEvents: Tinubu takes office amid worsening insecurity

data.note: ACLED: 9,662 killed in violent incidents. NBS/OCCRP: 7,568 kidnapped (Jul 2023–Jun 2024). Borno (2,143 killed), Zamfara (1,347), Katsina (1,306), Kaduna (813) worst-hit states. Q1 alone: 2,336 killed (26 per day). NBS survey found 2.2 million abduction incidents and ₦2.23 trillion in ransoms paid (May 2023–Apr 2024).
keyEvents: NBS reveals 2.2 million kidnap incidents in 12 months
Kaduna, Benue, Katsina, Plateau, Borno worst affected

data.note: Sahara Reporters/Intersociety: 6,800 killed and 5,402 kidnapped in first 6 months (H1 2025). Zamfara worst hit: 1,088 deaths, 1,755 abducted. Q1 alone: 1,420 killed, 537 kidnapped. NHRC April 2025: 570 killings, 278 kidnappings in single month. Nov 2025: 402+ kidnapped across 4 states including 303 schoolchildren from St. Mary's Catholic School.
keyEvents: 6,800 killed in H1 alone — on pace to exceed 2024
St. Mary's Catholic School: 303 children kidnapped (Nov)
Kebbi school attack: 25 schoolgirls kidnapped, vice-principal killed (Nov)

data.note: Ongoing violence. Jan: 163 Christians abducted in Kaduna. Feb: 162 killed in Kwara, 176 women/children abducted by Boko Haram. Mar: Palm Sunday massacre in Jos (28+ killed). Plateau killings in Wase LGA.
keyEvents: Jos Palm Sunday massacre: 28+ killed in Angwan Rukuba by gunmen on motorcycles (Mar 29)
162 killed in Woro and Nuku village executions, Kwara (Feb 3)
176 women and children abducted by Boko Haram (Feb 14)
163 Christians kidnapped from 2 churches in Kaduna (Jan 18)
Zamfara Air Force airstrike kills 20 villagers (Jan)
Plateau: killings in Zurak and Sabon Gari, Wase LGA (Feb)
Oro-Ago: vigilante commander killed, NSCDC office attacked (Mar 25)
48-hour curfew imposed on Jos North after Palm Sunday attack

summary.tinubuEra: More than 10,000 people have been killed and thousands kidnapped since Tinubu took office in May 2023. Since January 2023, at least 816 pupils have been taken in 22 school attacks.
summary.kidnappingIndustry: Kidnapping has become a multi-billion naira industry. The NBS estimated ₦2.23 trillion ($1.4 billion) paid in ransoms in a single year (May 2023–Apr 2024).
